Press enter or click to view image in full size

For years, `GET` has been the standard HTTP method for retrieving
resources. It works well for simple requests, but modern applications often need advanced searching, filtering, reporting, and analytics.
Many developers solved this by sending `POST` requests with a JSON body
for read-only operations. Although it works, it doesn’t accurately
express the intent of the request.
To address this gap, the HTTP Working Group introduced the HTTP
QUERY method in RFC 9553. It allows clients to send complex query
criteria in the request body while preserving the semantics of a read
operation.
What Is the HTTP QUERY Method?
The `QUERY` method is a new HTTP request method designed for complex
read operations.
Unlike `GET`, which carries filters in the URL, `QUERY` allows
structured request bodies.
QUERY /products HTTP/1.1
Content-Type: application/json{
"category": "Laptop",
"brands": ["Apple", "Dell"],
"price": {
"min": 1000,
"max": 3000
}
}
The server evaluates the query and returns matching resources without
modifying data.
Why Was QUERY Introduced?
`GET` is excellent for simple resource retrieval.
GET /products?category=Laptop
GET /products?page=2However, URLs become difficult to maintain when search criteria become
deeply nested or contain many conditions.
Developers often switched to `POST` because it accepts a request body.
POST /products/searchAlthough practical, `POST` is primarily intended for creating or
processing resources. Using it for read operations makes the API less
expressive.
The `QUERY` method fills this gap by combining the flexibility of a
request body with the semantics of a read-only operation.
GET vs POST vs QUERY
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Implementing QUERY in Express
Express does not currently expose a dedicated routing helper such as
`app.query()`.
You can still handle the method by checking `req.method`.
const express = require("express");const app = express();
app.use(express.json());
app.use((req, res, next) => {
if (req.method === "QUERY" && req.path === "/products") {
return res.json({
message: "QUERY request received",
filters: req.body
});
}
next();
});
Example request:
QUERY /products
Content-Type: application/json{
"category": "Laptop"
}
Current Support
The QUERY method is standardized in RFC 9553, but framework support is
still evolving.

• Express has no built-in `app.query()` method.

• Most HTTP clients support custom methods.

• Some proxies and API gateways may require additional configuration.
When Should You Use QUERY?
Use QUERY for:

• Advanced search APIs

• Analytics dashboards

• Report generation

• Business intelligence queries

• Complex filtering
Continue using GET for simple resource retrieval.
Conclusion
The HTTP QUERY method addresses a long-standing gap in the HTTP ecosystem by providing a standardized way to perform complex read operations using a request body. Instead of relying on POST as a workaround, developers now have a method that more accurately represents the intent of advanced search and retrieval requests.
Although support across frameworks and tools is still evolving, understanding the QUERY method today will help you design cleaner, more expressive APIs and prepare you for its broader adoption in the future.
